0: <title>Greenhouse gas emission intensity based on lifetime milk production of dairy animals, as affected by ration-balancing program </title>
M. R. GargA,C, , P. L. Sherasia^, B. T. Phondba^ and H. P. S. Makkar

Animal Nutrition Group, National Dairy Development Board, Anand-388 001, Gujarat, India. BAnimal Production and Health Division, Food and Agriculture Organisation of the United Nations (FAO), Viale delle Terme di Caracalla, 00153 Rome, Italy. Corresponding author. Email: mrgarg@nddb.coop

Abstract. Smallholder dairying in India and other developing countries relies on low- and medium-productive animals, and the feeding is mainly based on crop residues and other agro-industrial by-products. The diets are generally nutritionally imbalanced, resulting in productive and reproductive inefficiencies. This also negatively affects the emission intensity (Ei). For the past 3 years, the National Dairy Development Board of India has been implementing large-scale ration-balancing (RB) program in field animals. The effect of feeding balanced rations on Ei was explored. A cradle to farm-gate life-cycle assessment, taking into account the lifespan milk production, was conducted on 63 540 lactating COWS and 550 buffaloes in northern, southern, eastern and western India. The life-cycle assessment boundary included feed production, enteric fermentation and manure management during various stages of life. On the basis of economic allocation, emissions of methane (CH4) from enteric fermentation, CH4 from manure management, nitrous oxide from manure management and greenhouse gas (GHG), i.e. carbon dioxide (CO2), CH4 and nitrous oxide from feed production, contributed 69.9%, 6.3%, 9.6% and 14.2% in cows, and 71.6%, 7.4%, 12.6% and 8.4% in buffaloes, respectively, to the baseline (before RB) lifetime total GHG emissions. Average Ei based on economic, mass and digestibility allocation for 'baseline versus after RB' were 1.6 versus 1.1, 1.8 versus 1.2 and 1.7 versus 1.2 kg CO2-equivalent/kg fat and protein-corrected milk in COWS and 2.3 versus 1.5, 2.5 versus 1.6 and 2.4 versus 1.5 kg CO2-equivalent/kg fat and protein-corrected milk in buffaloes, respectively. Feeding- balanced rations significantly improved milk production, but reduced Ei of milk on lifetime basis by 31.2% and 34.7% in cows and buffaloes, respectively. Implementation of RB program has shown considerable potential to reduce GHG emission intensity under smallholding dairy production system of India.
Additional keywords: balanced feeding, greenhouse gases, life-cycle assessment, smallholder, sustainability.
Received 14 September 2015, accepted 21 January 2016, published online 3 May 2016

3: Livestock production is responsible for ~14.5% of the global anthropogenic greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. The sector, therefore, is widely challenged to reduce its impact on climate change. Dairy cattle producing milk, meat and non-edible products (e.g. manure) are responsible for ~30% GHG emissions (Gerber et al. 2013). About half of these emissions are related to enteric fermentation. Other important sources of GHG emissions are feed production and manure management.

Life-cycle assessment (LCA) is one of the methods used to assess the environmental impacts of a product throughout the product chain (ISO 14040 2006). Several LCA studies have focussed on dairy in the past decade (Guinard et al. 2009). Much of this research has been conducted in Australia (Lundie et al. 2003), Scandinavia (Eide 2002) and Western European countries (Hospido et al. 2003). Most LCA studies have allocated GHG emissions from livestock to marketable outputs.

FAO (2010) reported that emissions of GHG per kilogram fat and protein-corrected milk declined exponentially as annual milk production per cow increased. On the basis of this report, Gerber et al. (2011) reported that increasing annual milk production per cow could lower emissions of GHG in systems with low milk yield per cow, such as small-scale mixed crop-livestock systems, also known as smallholder production system. However, in the smallholder system, livestock are often kept not only to produce milk or meat, but also to produce fertiliser, provide draught power and act as capital asset (Weiler et al. 2014).

Livestock production systems in India and other developing countries are diverse and are driven by local resource supply. In smallholder crop-livestock production systems of India, dairy animals are usually fed one or two locally available concentrates to supplement grasses, crop residues and other agro-industrial by- products. This often leads to feeding of an imbalanced ration, resulting in productive and reproductive inefficiencies. Makkar

(2013) reported that most developing countries have shortage of good-quality feed ingredients. In addition, feeding imbalanced rations is widely prevalent in smallholder dairy production systems. As a result, average daily milk production of cows and buffaloes in India is very low, being 6.8 kg/crossbred cow (exotic), 2.5 kg/indigenous cow (zebu) and 4.9 kg/water buffalo (DAH, D&F 2014), which could be responsible for higher emission intensity (Ei). One of the possible ways to reduce Ei is to increase productivity through feeding balanced rations using locally available feed resources. Bayat and Shingfield (2012) and Hristov et al. (2013) also reported that the most relevant methane mitigation strategy for smallholder mixed crop-livestock system in developing countries is to increase individual animal productivity, by providing nutritionally balanced feeds.

The National Dairy Development Board (NDDB) of India has developed a user-friendly ration-balancing (RB) software for preparing a least-cost balanced ration for dairy animals under field conditions. For the past 3 years, the NDDB has been implementing the RB program, focusing in 15 major milk- producing states of India and envisages covering 2.7 million lactating cows and buffaloes in 40 000 villages by the year 2018-2019. There is hardly any information available on GHG emission intensity of dairy animals in developing countries on the basis of LCA model, by collecting data on actual feed intake and milk production by the field animals. An attempt was made to collect baseline information on dairy animals by covering more than 0.3 million lactating cows (Bos indicus, Bos taurus) and buffaloes (Bubalus bubalis), and an assessment of the impact of feeding balanced rations on GHG emission reduction was undertaken; the results are reported in the present study.

7: The NDDB developed an RB program (RBP) that is a windows-based internet-linked application designed to assess the prevailing nutritional status of an animal's diet versus its nutrient requirements. Both sets of information are used to determine a least-cost ration with the available feed resources and a mineral mixture. The software is compatible with desktops, laptops and netbooks, and can be used on personal digital assistants for areas devoid of internet connectivity.

The RBP comprises a feed-data library and various 'nutrition masters'. To create the feed-data library, a wide range of feed ingredients such as green and dry forages, tree leaves, grains, oil cakes and agro-industrial by-products were collected from different agro-ecological zones of the country and analysed for chemical composition. Simultaneously, existing national and international feeding standards for nutrient requirement of growing, lactating and pregnant animals were used to create various 'nutrition masters' of nutrient requirements (Kearl 1982; NRC 2001).
8: The RBP was implemented using individual animal profiles, and data thus generated were based on individual animals. Through various implementing agencies with adequate

infrastructure and manpower (addressed as end implementing agencies, EIAs), NDDB initiated multi-state implementation of RBP on a large scale across India. The EIAs included dairy cooperative societies (DCS), service-providing organisations, state animal husbandry departments, producer companies and non-government organisations. The NDDB imparted training to identified extension workers of the implementing agencies, on the RBP software in regional language.
9: Prospective extension workers submitted a completed application form to the DCS, which then shortlisted and recommended potential candidates for a personnel interview. More than 3000 extension workers were identified and trained in groups of 10-15 for 2 weeks, comprising 1 week in a class room and 1 week on field training. The DCS technical officers and supervisory staff regularly monitored the work completed by the extension workers and organised refresher programs whenever required. Each extension worker covered ~100 animals in one or two adjacent villages. Each extension worker regularly visited the milk producers' farm at a monthly interval. In addition, if there was any change in the supply or availability of feeds, milk producers contacted the extension workers and a ration was re-formulated.
10: <header>Steps for formulating a least-cost BR Registration of animals </header>

Animals identified for the RBP were ear tagged with a unique 12-digit number. Details of the animal, such as species, breed, age, milking status (lactating/dry), calving number, last calving date and pregnancy status were recorded in the RBP software. The owner's profile (i.e. name, father's name, age, village, village institution, 'tehsil' (zone), district and state) was also recorded. After collecting all the information, animals were registered in the software.
11: After registration, the animal's daily feed intake including refusals, daily milk yield, milk fat level, bodyweight (BW) and pregnancy status were also recorded in the software. The software calculated the nutrient requirement of the animals, on the basis of the inputs provided. Also, on the basis of the prevailing feeding practices, nutrient status of the feed offered to animals in terms of metabolisable energy (ME), crude protein (CP) and essential minerals was assessed, in comparison to the requirements.

On the basis of the chemical composition of available feed resources, maximum quantity available for feeding and nutritional constraints, the software computed a least-cost BR, which was provided to milk producers in a local language. Extension workers visited milk producers at regular intervals or whenever there was change in the availability of feed ingredients. Complete details on infrastructure and tools required for implementation of RBP are available in literature (FAO 2012; Garg et al. 2013).
Life-cycle assessment The LCA was performed in compliance with the ISO standards (ISO 14040 2006; ISO 14044 2006) and the IDF (2010)

guidelines on a common carbon footprint approach for dairy. The stages of LCA methodology included in the study were goal, scope and system boundary, life-cycle inventory and impact assessment. These stages are described in brief below.

The software calculated the nutrient requirement of the animals, on the basis of the inputs provided. Life-cycle assessment The LCA was performed in compliance with the ISO standards (ISO 14040 2006; ISO 14044 2006) and the IDF (2010)

guidelines on a common carbon footprint approach for dairy. The stages of LCA methodology included in the study were goal, scope and system boundary, life-cycle inventory and impact assessment. These stages are described in brief below.
13: The goals of the current study were (1) to calculate the Ei on lifetime milk production of dairy animals fed traditional rations, under smallholding dairy production systems and; (2) to evaluate the impact of feeding a nutritionally balanced ration on Ei throughout the lifespan of dairy animals. The environmental impacts on a global scale, such as emissions of GHGs, are generally related to a product-based functional unit (Haas et al. 2000). In current study, the chosen functional unit was '1 kg of fat and protein-corrected milk (FPCM) leaving the farm gate'. Total milk yield was corrected to 4.0% fat and 3.3% protein for cow milk, using the following equation:
Buffalo milk was corrected to 4.0% fat and 3.1% protein, using the following equation developed by Di Palo (1992):
14: The scope of LCA included two separate processes, i.e. off- farm and on-farm processes. Off-farm process included off-farm activities related to dairying, i.e. cultivation, production, processing and transportation of external feeds to the farm. The GHGs emitted from cultivation, use of fossil fuel, electricity, and fertilisers and pesticides during production of external feeds were included in this process. Region-wise transportation of raw material (i.e. feed ingredients) to cattle

feed-processing plant, and finished product (i.e. cattle feed) to smallholder dairy farms were considered while calculating GHG emissions. Similarly, GHGs emitted from transportation of purchased food crop by-products (e.g. brans and oilseed meals) directly by the farmers were also included in off-farm process. On-farm processes include the farm activities such as husbandry and management of dairy animals, manure management, inputs involved in on-farm feed production and energy used in the form of fossil fuel. The system boundary defines the processes to be included in the LCA. A 'cradle-to-farm gate' analysis for computing Ei of lifetime milk production was applied, which meant emissions of GHGs were assessed for all processes involved until the milk left the smallholder dairy farm (Fig. 1). This Ei assessment followed the attributional approach, which implies estimating the environmental impact of an existing situation, under current production and market conditions (Thomassen et al. 2008).
15: The scope of LCA included two separate processes, i.e. off- farm and on-farm processes. Off-farm process included off-farm activities related to dairying, i.e. cultivation, production, processing and transportation of external feeds to the farm. The GHGs emitted from cultivation, use of fossil fuel, electricity, and fertilisers and pesticides during production of external feeds were included in this process. Region-wise transportation of raw material (i.e. feed ingredients) to cattle

feed-processing plant, and finished product (i.e. cattle feed) to smallholder dairy farms were considered while calculating GHG emissions. Similarly, GHGs emitted from transportation of purchased food crop by-products (e.g. brans and oilseed meals) directly by the farmers were also included in off-farm process. On-farm processes include the farm activities such as husbandry and management of dairy animals, manure management, inputs involved in on-farm feed production and energy used in the form of fossil fuel. The system boundary defines the processes to be included in the LCA. A 'cradle-to-farm gate' analysis for computing Ei of lifetime milk production was applied, which meant emissions of GHGs were assessed for all processes involved until the milk left the smallholder dairy farm (Fig. 1). This Ei assessment followed the attributional approach, which implies estimating the environmental impact of an existing situation, under current production and market conditions (Thomassen et al. 2008).
16: Three main allocation methods exist in ISO 14044 (2006), namely economic, physical (mass) and system expansion allocation. In the present LCA, economic and mass allocations were applied as per ISO 14044 (2006), whereas, digestibility allocation was applied as per IDF (2010). During food-crop production, human-edible products (such as, for example, grains of rice, maize) and non-edible crop by-products (e.g. bran) or residues (e.g. straw, stover etc.) are produced. The crop by-products or residues not edible to humans were mostly used for livestock feeding by the farmers. Therefore, to account emissions related to crop by-products or residues not edible to humans, an allocation of emissions related to feed production on either economic, mass or digestibility basis was necessary.
In situations where grains (e.g. maize) were part of the cattle feed, the allocation factors for respective grain(s) were applied to the GHG emissions from feed production. No allocation factor
Fig. 1. Cradle-to-farm gate system boundary of milk production.

was applied to the GHG emissions from green-fodder production, as the entire quantity produced was used for feeding animals. For computing mass allocation, the proportion of grains, crop by- products or residues in total quantity of biomass produced after deducting harvesting losses were considered. For example, mass allocation factor for rice grain was assigned as 32.2% after considering the proportions for rice husk, rice polish (milling adjusted to yield <5% bran) and straw as 8.5%, 1.7% and 57.6%, respectively. Economic allocation was computed on the basis of proportion of grains, crop by-products or residues in total economic value of biomass of the respective crop. The digestibility values of different grains, crop by-products or residues for ruminants were obtained from Feedipedia (2015). The proportion of digestible dry matter (DM) of each feed ingredient in total quantity of digestible DM produced by the respective crop was considered while computing digestible allocation. The details of allocation factors used in the present study for the feed ingredients are presented in Table 1.

total GHG emissions of farms. In the present assessment, only milk was considered as an output of the system and, thus, all GHG emissions were allocated to milk, i.e. 100% allocation to milk. Since ancient times, cow has been considered as a symbol of wealth, and has been honoured and worshiped in the Indian culture. In most of the states of India, slaughter of cow is prohibited by law. Slaughter of unfit animals such as unproductive buffalo (i.e. incapable of or ceased producing milk) is strictly controlled by authorities. Considering associated cultural values of bovines and existing anti-slaughter laws, old COWS and buffaloes during their unproductive phase of lifespan were not sent for slaughter by the farmers. Due to this reason, meat was not considered as a co-product of the dairy system in the present assessment. The manure was mostly used as fertiliser by the farmers in different regions and the quantity of manure used as fertiliser was difficult to determine due to lack of records at farmers' end. Therefore, in this assessment, manure was not considered as co-product of the system. Other functions of livestock, such as the use for dowry and a sign of identity and wealth, cannot be appropriately and meaningfully quantified in economic terms.
17: Three main allocation methods exist in ISO 14044 (2006), namely economic, physical (mass) and system expansion allocation. In the present LCA, economic and mass allocations were applied as per ISO 14044 (2006), whereas, digestibility allocation was applied as per IDF (2010). During food-crop production, human-edible products (such as, for example, grains of rice, maize) and non-edible crop by-products (e.g. bran) or residues (e.g. straw, stover etc.) are produced. The crop by-products or residues not edible to humans were mostly used for livestock feeding by the farmers. Therefore, to account emissions related to crop by-products or residues not edible to humans, an allocation of emissions related to feed production on either economic, mass or digestibility basis was necessary.
In situations where grains (e.g. maize) were part of the cattle feed, the allocation factors for respective grain(s) were applied to the GHG emissions from feed production. No allocation factor
Fig. 1. Cradle-to-farm gate system boundary of milk production.

was applied to the GHG emissions from green-fodder production, as the entire quantity produced was used for feeding animals. For computing mass allocation, the proportion of grains, crop by- products or residues in total quantity of biomass produced after deducting harvesting losses were considered. For example, mass allocation factor for rice grain was assigned as 32.2% after considering the proportions for rice husk, rice polish (milling adjusted to yield <5% bran) and straw as 8.5%, 1.7% and 57.6%, respectively. Economic allocation was computed on the basis of proportion of grains, crop by-products or residues in total economic value of biomass of the respective crop. The digestibility values of different grains, crop by-products or residues for ruminants were obtained from Feedipedia (2015). The proportion of digestible dry matter (DM) of each feed ingredient in total quantity of digestible DM produced by the respective crop was considered while computing digestible allocation. The details of allocation factors used in the present study for the feed ingredients are presented in Table 1.

total GHG emissions of farms. In the present assessment, only milk was considered as an output of the system and, thus, all GHG emissions were allocated to milk, i.e. 100% allocation to milk. Since ancient times, cow has been considered as a symbol of wealth, and has been honoured and worshiped in the Indian culture. In most of the states of India, slaughter of cow is prohibited by law. Slaughter of unfit animals such as unproductive buffalo (i.e. incapable of or ceased producing milk) is strictly controlled by authorities. Considering associated cultural values of bovines and existing anti-slaughter laws, old COWS and buffaloes during their unproductive phase of lifespan were not sent for slaughter by the farmers. Due to this reason, meat was not considered as a co-product of the dairy system in the present assessment. The manure was mostly used as fertiliser by the farmers in different regions and the quantity of manure used as fertiliser was difficult to determine due to lack of records at farmers' end. Therefore, in this assessment, manure was not considered as co-product of the system. Other functions of livestock, such as the use for dowry and a sign of identity and wealth, cannot be appropriately and meaningfully quantified in economic terms.
22: In farms studied in the present assessment, on-farm chilling of milk was not undertaken. Instead, milk was carried to the village- level dairy cooperative societies where bulk-milk coolers were operated by the milk unions. The milk collected from farmers was further sold by dairy cooperatives to district-level milk unions. In this case, the milk unions were the ultimate dairy processors. Therefore, chilling of milk was considered under post-farm processing of milk. Most of the dairy farms in the present study belonged to small and marginal farmers that followed traditional dairy husbandry practices, such as, for example, milking by hand being preferred over machine milking. Therefore, direct GHG emissions from on-farm electricity use (mainly for water heating, milk chilling and milking machine) could be lower for investigated farms. Divya et al. (2012) also reported that for small, medium and large dairy farms in India, overall electrical energy use was 0.43 MJ/animal.day, representing 0.25% of total input energy of farms. For this reason, GHG emissions from on-farm electricity use were not accounted in the present study. In current study, production processes of medicines, machinery and buildings were excluded from the analysis (de Boer 2003). The cleaning agents, infrastructure and maintenance were also excluded from the model. The main reason for such exclusion was lack of precise information for these inputs. In addition, embedded GHG emissions from these sources were expected to remain constant regardless of the two scenarios (i.e. before and after RB); thus, such exclusion did not affect the results of the present study. Carbon sequestration in soil was excluded, not only due to lack of reliable data but also because, on the basis of the guidelines, arable lands under cultivation for more than 20 years might be in equilibrium (IPCC 2006).
To create life-cycle inventory, a mixture of primary and secondary data sources was used for calculating the Ei of milk production. Primary data regarding animals (species, breed, age,

bodyweight of lactating COWS and buffaloes; calculated using Shaeffer's formula (Sastry et al. 1982; Garg et al. 2013) as follows: bodyweight (kg) = {[(heart girth (cm)/2.54)2 X length of the body (cm)/2.54)/300] X 0.4536}, feeding practices (feed ingredients used, nutrient intakes and requirements), daily milk production and milk fat yield were collected from each and every milk producer through farm visits by the trained extension workers. Individual milk producers were visited by extension workers for collecting the primary data between January and December 2013. Present study involved 163 540 lactating COWS and 163 550 buffaloes in northern (Haryana, Punjab, Uttar Pradesh states), southern (Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka, Kerala states), eastern (Bihar, Orissa, Madhya Pradesh states) and western (Gujarat, Maharashtra, Rajasthan states) regions of India. In our study, the sample size was very large, representing a complete cross-section of farming practices across the country. Majority of the milk producers under the study were practicing mixed crop-livestock systems, wherein cash and fodder crops were grown, and some dairy animals were kept.

The secondary technical data on average production parameters (Table 2), such as average lifespan of a cow and buffalo, age at first calving, average number of lactations, lactation length, dry period, inter-calving interval, bodyweight at birth, heifer (a mature cow that has never calved), dry period (time between halting of milk removal and the subsequent calving) and unproductive stage (mature cow that cannot be calved in future) of COWS and buffaloes in different regions, were obtained from the literature (Nivasarkar et al. 2000; Singh and Barwal 2010). Average lifespan of a cow and buffalo was considered to be 12.5 years for all regions. On the basis of our personal communication with the farmers of different regions, where RB program has been implemented, we considered that after feeding balanced rations, average age at first calving and average dry period per lactation were reduced by 180 days and 90 days in cows, and 360 days and 90 days in buffaloes, respectively. Similarly, average number of lactations and average days in milk per lactation were considered to increase by 2.0 and 30 days respectively in both the
species, after feeding balanced rations in different regions of India.

26: On the basis of the individual feed ingredients used in RBP, region-specific average compositions of rations were formulated for lactating cows and buffaloes separately (Table 3). Feed ingredients used as concentrates were compound cattle feed, maize grain (Zea mays), wheat grain (Triticum aestivum), rice grain (Oryza sativa), jowar grain (Sorghum bicolour), whole cotton seeds (Gossypium spp.), cottonseed cake, rapeseed cake (Brassica napus), groundnut cake (Arachis hypogaea), til cake (Sesamum indicum), rice bran and wheat bran in different regions. Generally, these concentrate feed ingredients were fed to lactating COWS and buffaloes only. Very little or no concentrate feed ingredients were fed to heifer, dry-period and unproductive stages of cows and buffaloes. Wheat straw, jowar straw and paddy straw were generally used as dry fodder for feeding to all stages of COWS and buffaloes. As sources of green fodder, various fodders, such as jowar, oat (Avena sativa), maize, lucerne (Medicago sativa) and berseem (Trifolium alexandrinum), were used. Local grasses and sugarcane tops (Saccharum officinarum) were also used S green fodder in different regions.

Generally, all these feed ingredients were used before and after RB, although the proportions used were changed after RB. A mineral mixture was included in the formulation of balanced rations, because it was commonly not used or was improperly used by the milk producers across the country.

37: The extension workers measured daily milk yield 5 days before and 30 days after feeding a balanced ration, by visiting the farms of individual farmers. Milk samples were collected and analysed for milk fat content by Milko Tester (Rajasthan Electronics and Instruments Ltd., Jaipur, India), located at the village dairy cooperative society (all dairy cooperative societies have milk-testing facilities at a village level in India). Feed ingredients fed to animals were from home-grown and purchased feed ingredients.
38: The most important GHGs related to smallholder dairy systems were carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20). GHG emissions from feed production included CO2, CH4 and N20 emitted during cultivation, production, processing and transportation of feeds. Emissions of CH4 from enteric fermentation, CH4 and N2O from manure management and GHGs from feed production were combined to arrive at total GHG emissions of farms. All these GHG emissions were estimated using Tier 2 approach of the IPCC (2006) guidelines.

Emissions of CO2 resulted from the combustion of fossil fuel to power farm machinery (such as tractors), electricity, fertilisers and pesticides used in feed production, and from transportation of feeds. The CO2 emissions from diesel were computed using the emission factor (EF) 3.25 kg CO2-eq./L, as per Berglund et al. (2009), based on heating value and specific gravity of the fuel. For computing CO2 emissions from electricity used in feed production, the direct equivalent method accounting one unit of secondary energy provided from non-combustible sources as one unit of primary energy, that is 1 kWh of electricity accounted as 3.6 MJ of primary energy, was followed; the average electricity consumption for feed production (equivalents of MJ) was multiplied by EF of 0.47 kg CO2-eq./MJ (Berglund et al. 2009). GHG emissions from synthetic fertilisers were estimated using EF of 4.0, 3.3 and 1.0 kg CO2-eq./kg of N, phosphorus (P) and potassium (K), respectively, whereas for pesticides EF was 17.3 kg CO2-eq./kg active ingredient (Berglund et al. 2009). GHG emissions from transportation were estimated using EF of 0.11 kg CO2-eq./km.t of feed ingredients transported through trucks (FeedPrint 2015). CH4 emissions from feed production were mainly attributed to cultivation of paddy rice. Average CH4 emission from rice cultivation was estimated as 183 kg/ha (IPCC 2006, eqn 5.1). Direct N2O emissions from application of synthetic nitrogenous fertilisers during feed production were estimated using default EF of 0.01 kg N2O-N/kg N applied (IPCC 2006). Indirect N2O emissions from application of organic and inorganic fertilisers were estimated as per IPCC (2006).

Methane was also produced by ruminants mainly as a by- product of enteric fermentation. For estimating enteric CH4 emissions, average daily feed intake expressed as gross energy intake was calculated from the diet for cattle and buffalo. Enteric CH4 emissions were estimated as a function of intake, using the conversion factor (Ym = 6.5% of gross energy intake) and

computing EF for each animal category (IPCC 2006, table 10.12, eqn 10.21). CH4 emissions from manure were estimated as a proportion of the maximum CH4 potential of manure volatile solids (VS) excreted (IPCC 2006, eqn 10.23). Estimated excretions of manure VS were obtained using the DM intake and diet digestibility (IPCC 2006, eqn 10.24). The proportion of the maximum CH4 potential that was emitted from manure VS was computed using specific CH4 conversion factor for solid- manure storage system, i.e. 5.0% (IPCC 2006, table 10.17). The CH4 conversion factor was selected considering an annual average ambient temperature >28°C for India.

Emissions of N20 from manure were derived after calculating N excretion, which was estimated as the total N in feed intake minus the N in milk and that was retained in animals (calf and heifer growth). Direct N2O emissions from the stored manure were estimated using the corresponding default EF for solid- manure storage system, i.e. 0.005 kg N2O-N/kg N excreted (IPCC 2006, table 10.21, eqn 10.25). Volatilisation of NH3 and leaching of NO contributed to indirect emissions of N2O. Losses of N through volatilisation (NH3 and NOX) from solid-manure storage system were considered as 30% (IPCC 2006, table 10.22). Loss of N through leaching was assumed as 4% of N excreted from solid-storage system (i.e. <5% of N excreted as per IPCC 2006). The fraction of N that volatilised as NH3 and NO under different conditions was considered as 0.10 and 0.20 kg/kg of N from synthetic or organic fertilisers applied respectively, and N loss by leaching was considered as 0.30 kg/kg of N applied to soil (IPCC 2006, table 11.3). Default EF for N2O emissions was 0.01 and 0.0075 kg N2O-N/kg of N volatilised and leached, respectively (IPCC 2006, table 11.3).

42: The impact assessment was conducted according to the IPCC (2007). We used LCA to calculate emissions of CO2, CH4 and N2O from the different stages along the production chain, up to the moment that milk leaves the farm gate. Total GHG emissions included CO2, CH4 and N20 from feed production (cultivation, production, processing, transportation), enteric CH4 and, manure CH4 and N2O. All the GHGs were summed up on the basis of their equivalent factor in terms of CO2-eq. The characterisation factors for a 100-year horizon were 1 for CO2, 25 for CH4 and 298 for N2O (IPCC 2007). The Ei of milk was computed for animals fed on either traditional imbalanced rations (i.e. before RB) or balanced rations (i.e. after RB) throughout their lifespan.
43: Statistical significance was considered at P < 0.05 and paired Student's t-test was used as per Snedecor and Cochran (1986), using SPSS package (SPSS 1999).
44: After feeding balanced rations, average DMI (kg/cow.day) reduced (P < 0.05) from 2.6 to 1.9 (heifer), 13.3 to 11.1 (lactation), 7.9 to 5.6 (dry period) and 6.1 to 4.1 (unproductive stage) in cows, whereas DMI (kg/buffalo.day) reduced (P<0.05) from 3.8 to 3.1 (heifer), 15.1 to 13.0 (lactation), 12.4 to 10.2 (dry period) and 8.8 to 6.9 (unproductive stage) in buffaloes

(Table 4). Significant 0.05) reduction in NI was observed in heifer (29.3% and 11.8%), lactation (17.6% and 7.4%), dry period (30.8% and 11.7%) and unproductive stage (33.5% nd19.3%) of cows and buffaloes respectively, after feeding balanced rations.

After feeding balanced rations, total FPCM yield in lifespan of COWS and buffaloes increased by 75.0% (P < 0.05) and 54.3% (P < 0.05) in the northern region, 52.3% (P < 0.05) and 95.4% in the southern region, 75.0% (P < 0.05) and 71.5% (P < 0.05) in the eastern region and, 56.3% (P < 0.05) and 82.8% (P < 0.05) in the western region, respectively.
45: After feeding balanced rations, average DMI (kg/cow.day) reduced (P < 0.05) from 2.6 to 1.9 (heifer), 13.3 to 11.1 (lactation), 7.9 to 5.6 (dry period) and 6.1 to 4.1 (unproductive stage) in cows, whereas DMI (kg/buffalo.day) reduced (P<0.05) from 3.8 to 3.1 (heifer), 15.1 to 13.0 (lactation), 12.4 to 10.2 (dry period) and 8.8 to 6.9 (unproductive stage) in buffaloes

(Table 4). Significant 0.05) reduction in NI was observed in heifer (29.3% and 11.8%), lactation (17.6% and 7.4%), dry period (30.8% and 11.7%) and unproductive stage (33.5% nd19.3%) of cows and buffaloes respectively, after feeding balanced rations.

After feeding balanced rations, total FPCM yield in lifespan of COWS and buffaloes increased by 75.0% (P < 0.05) and 54.3% (P < 0.05) in the northern region, 52.3% (P < 0.05) and 95.4% in the southern region, 75.0% (P < 0.05) and 71.5% (P < 0.05) in the eastern region and, 56.3% (P < 0.05) and 82.8% (P < 0.05) in the western region, respectively.
46: Before RB, emissions of total CH4 (enteric and manure), i.e. national average, were 117.4 kg, 166.2 kg and 58.1 kg, which were reduced (P < 0.05) to 106.0 kg, 116.1 kg and 19.7 kg kg in heifer, dry-period and unproductive stages of cows, respectively (Table 5). On feeding balanced rations, average total CH4 emissions were reduced (P < 0.05) from 151.7 to 118.1 kg, 178.3 to 133.1 kg and 151.8 to 118.8 kg in heifer, dry-period and unproductive stages of buffaloes, respectively (Table 6). However, in lactation stage, total CH4 emissions were increased (P < 0.05) from 397.5 to 600.9 kg and 366.2 to 561.4 kg in COWS and buffaloes, respectively.

After feeding balanced rations, reductions (P < 0.05) in total N2O emissions from manure management were observed of 50.0%, 47.6% and 83.3%, in heifer, dry-period and unproductive stages of cows, respectively. Similarly, total N2O emissions from manure management were reduced (P < 0.05) by 50.0%, 38.0% and 31.2% in heifer, dry-period and unproductive stages of buffaloes, respectively. However, total N2O emissions from manure management were increased (P < 0.05) by 17.8% and 34.0% in the lactation stage of cows and buffaloes, respectively.
After feeding balanced rations, national average CH4 emission from enteric fermentation was increased (P < 0.05) from 16.7 to 19.7 metric tonnes, and from 15.4 to 17.6 metric tonnes of

CO2-eq during a lifespan of a cow and buffalo, respectively (Tables 7, 8). Similarly, average CH4 emission from manure management was also increased (P < 0.05) by 20.0% and 12.5% during a lifespan of a cow and buffalo, respectively. Average N20 emissions from manure management were reduced (P < 0.05) by 17.4% and 7.4% during a lifespan of cow and buffalo, respectively, after feeding balanced rations. Feeding of balanced rations throughout a lifespan of cow and buffalo increased total GHG emissions (on an economic-allocation basis) by 12.8% and 12.6%, respectively.

Before RB, the Ei (kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM), on the basis of economic allocation, during a lifespan of a cow ranged from 1.4 in the western region to 2.9 in the eastern region, with a national average of 1.6. After RB, the Ei (kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM) ranged from 1.0 in the western region to 1.9 in the eastern region, with a national average of 1.1, which indicates a significant reduction in Ei by 31.2% (P < 0.05) on feeding balanced rations to dairy COWS under field conditions. On the basis of mass and digestibility allocations, the average Ei during a lifespan of a cow reduced from 1.8 to 1.2 (P < 0.05) kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM and from 1.7 to 1.2 (P < 0.05) kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM respectively, after feeding balanced rations. Similarly, on the basis of economic, mass and digestibility allocations, the average Ei after feeding balanced rations during a lifespan of a buffalo reduced from 2.3 to 1.5 (P<0.05), 2.5 to 1.6 < 0.05) and 2.4 to 1.5 <0.05) kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM, respectively (Table 8). After applying economic, mass and digestible allocations, the highest Ei was observed in the southern region of India, i.e. 6.1, 7.5 and 6.2 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM before RB, which reduced to 3.5, 4.1 and 3.5 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM respectively, after RB in buffaloes. The high Ei of buffalo milk may be due to the low productivity of buffaloes in the southern region, particularly in the state of Andhra Pradesh.

On feeding balanced rations, average total CH4 emissions were reduced (P < 0.05) from 151.7 to 118.1 kg, 178.3 to 133.1 kg and 151.8 to 118.8 kg in heifer, dry-period and unproductive stages of buffaloes, respectively (Table 6). However, in lactation stage, total CH4 emissions were increased (P < 0.05) from 397.5 to 600.9 kg and 366.2 to 561.4 kg in COWS and buffaloes, respectively.

After feeding balanced rations, reductions (P < 0.05) in total N2O emissions from manure management were observed of 50.0%, 47.6% and 83.3%, in heifer, dry-period and unproductive stages of cows, respectively. Similarly, total N2O emissions from manure management were reduced (P < 0.05) by 50.0%, 38.0% and 31.2% in heifer, dry-period and unproductive stages of buffaloes, respectively. However, total N2O emissions from manure management were increased (P < 0.05) by 17.8% and 34.0% in the lactation stage of cows and buffaloes, respectively.

60: <header>Table 7. Impact of ration balancing (RB) on emission intensity during a lifespan of a cow (weighted mean + s.e.) </header>

Lifespan includes heifer, lactation, dry period and unproductive stage in addition to replacement heifer. Greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from feed include carbon monoxide (CO2), methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20) emitted during cultivation, production, processing and transportation of feeds. CO2-eq, carbon dioxide equivalent; FPCM, fat and protein corrected milk; MT, metric tonnes; n, number of animals. Values within a row (comparing before RB with after RB within a region) followed by different letters are significantly different at P < 0.05
Table 8. Impact of ration balancing (RB) on emission intensity (weighted mean + s.e.) during a lifespan of a buffalo

Lifespan includes heifer, lactation, dry period and unproductive stage in addition to replacement heifer. GHG emissions from feed include carbon monoxide (CO2), methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20) emitted during cultivation, production, processing and transportation of feeds. Abbreviations as per Table 7. Values within a row (comparing before RB with after RB within a region) followed by different letters are significantly different at P < 0.05

their nutrient requirement was met in a cost-effective manner. At present, more than 1.0 million lactating COWS and buffaloes are covered by >13 000 extension workers across the country. Supply and availability of concentrates such as compound cattle feed, oil seed cake or meal and mineral mixture remained almost constant throughout the year. However, the seasonal changes observed in the locally available feed ingredients such as crop residues and other agro-industrial by- products, were taken care of while working out fresh formulations under such circumstances. Each extension worker regularly visited the farms of milk producers at regular intervals. Also, whenever there was a change in the supply and availability of feed ingredients, the extension workers were contacted by the milk producers. The extension workers visited such farms and re- formulated the rations according to the supply and availability of feed ingredients. For example, during summer season, recommended average balanced ration for buffaloes in the western region was cattle feed (18.5%), cottonseed cake (17.7%), lucerne fodder (23.4%), wheat straw (39.4%) and area-specific mineral mixture (ASMM, 1.0%). This ration was changed to cattle feed (19.5%), cottonseed cake (19.0%), sorghum fodder (22.3%), sorghum straw (38.2%) and ASMM (1.0%) during autumn, and cattle feed (18.5%), cottonseed cake (18.2%), lucerne fodder (20.7%), sorghum straw (41.5%) and ASMM (1.1%) during winter season. The balanced feeding program does not necessarily increase demand for feed resources. Instead, it helps in judicious utilisation of available feed resources, SO that when available raw materials are mixed in an appropriate proportion, they are able to meet the nutrient demands of animals. The basic aim of ration balancing is to fulfil the nutrient requirement of animals using locally available feed resources.

In the present study, due to large herds of cattle (n = 540) and buffalo (n = 53550), it was not possible to collect all information related to productivity of animals in various regions of the country. Due to this reason, the secondary technical data on production parameters (i.e. age at first calving, number of lactations, lactation length, dry period, inter-calving interval and bodyweight at different stages of life) were obtained from literature (Nivasarkar et al. 2000; Singh and Barwal 2010). The information on above production parameters was published by Indian Council of Agricultural Research and State Agricultural University after evaluation of various breeds of cattle and buffalo in different regions of the country. Therefore, in the present assessment, we considered this secondary information as a representative of large herds of cattle and buffalo.

Table 8. Impact of ration balancing (RB) on emission intensity (weighted mean + s.e.) during a lifespan of a buffalo

Lifespan includes heifer, lactation, dry period and unproductive stage in addition to replacement heifer. GHG emissions from feed include carbon monoxide (CO2), methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20) emitted during cultivation, production, processing and transportation of feeds. Abbreviations as per Table 7. Values within a row (comparing before RB with after RB within a region) followed by different letters are significantly different at P < 0.05

their nutrient requirement was met in a cost-effective manner. At present, more than 1.0 million lactating COWS and buffaloes are covered by >13 000 extension workers across the country. Supply and availability of concentrates such as compound cattle feed, oil seed cake or meal and mineral mixture remained almost constant throughout the year. However, the seasonal changes observed in the locally available feed ingredients such as crop residues and other agro-industrial by- products, were taken care of while working out fresh formulations under such circumstances. Each extension worker regularly visited the farms of milk producers at regular intervals. Also, whenever there was a change in the supply and availability of feed ingredients, the extension workers were contacted by the milk producers. The extension workers visited such farms and re- formulated the rations according to the supply and availability of feed ingredients. For example, during summer season, recommended average balanced ration for buffaloes in the western region was cattle feed (18.5%), cottonseed cake (17.7%), lucerne fodder (23.4%), wheat straw (39.4%) and area-specific mineral mixture (ASMM, 1.0%). This ration was changed to cattle feed (19.5%), cottonseed cake (19.0%), sorghum fodder (22.3%), sorghum straw (38.2%) and ASMM (1.0%) during autumn, and cattle feed (18.5%), cottonseed cake (18.2%), lucerne fodder (20.7%), sorghum straw (41.5%) and ASMM (1.1%) during winter season. The balanced feeding program does not necessarily increase demand for feed resources. Instead, it helps in judicious utilisation of available feed resources, SO that when available raw materials are mixed in an appropriate proportion, they are able to meet the nutrient demands of animals. The basic aim of ration balancing is to fulfil the nutrient requirement of animals using locally available feed resources.

In the present study, due to large herds of cattle (n = 540) and buffalo (n = 53550), it was not possible to collect all information related to productivity of animals in various regions of the country. Due to this reason, the secondary technical data on production parameters (i.e. age at first calving, number of lactations, lactation length, dry period, inter-calving interval and bodyweight at different stages of life) were obtained from literature (Nivasarkar et al. 2000; Singh and Barwal 2010). The information on above production parameters was published by Indian Council of Agricultural Research and State Agricultural University after evaluation of various breeds of cattle and buffalo in different regions of the country. Therefore, in the present assessment, we considered this secondary information as a representative of large herds of cattle and buffalo.

74: In the present study, higher DMI was observed before RB, reducing after RB. Similarly, NI was also reduced after RB in all stages of life of COWS and buffaloes. The results showed that the imbalanced feeding is widely prevalent in most of the regions of India. Similar findings were also observed by Tiwary et al. (2007). The higher DMI and NI before RB, as evident in the present study, could be due to overfeeding of protein and energy to cows and buffaloes in various states of India. Similar to these
findings, Garg et al. (2013) also reported overfeeding of protein and metabolisable energy in 70% of the animals in various states of India.

The improvement in FPCM in our study after feeding balanced rations could be due to increased rumen microbial protein synthesis, as a result of the more balanced nutrient supply similar to Garg et al. (2014). Supplementation of minerals to the diet of lactating COWS and buffaloes has been reported to enhance milk production and milk composition (Khochare et al. 2010). While balancing rations, the energy and protein that were otherwise fed in excess of the animal requirements, were restricted to fulfil animal nutrient requirement. Thus, the resultant DMI of cows and buffaloes was slightly lower (after RB) than after traditional feeding (before RB). Our results indicated that although there was a reduction in DMI of animals (after RB), the FPCM yield during a lifespan of cows and buffaloes increased significantly, which is mainly attributed to improved feed use efficiency in animals, similar to Garg et al. (2013).

(2013).
76: Balanced feeding reduced CH4 and N2O emissions in heifer, dry-period and unproductive stages of cows and buffaloes. In the present study, balancing of nutrients shifted the rumen fermentation pattern towards higher microbial cell production, which might have resulted in lower acetate and butyrate production, on account of higher propionate production, thereby reducing CH4 emissions. Similar to the present findings, Mohini and Singh (2010) also reported lower enteric CH4 emissions after balancing the rations of cows. In the lactation stage, in spite of the lower daily DMI and NI, total CH4 and N2O emissions were increased and this was mainly attributed to the increase in the number of lactations and days in milk after feeding balanced rations to COWS and buffaloes.

In the present study, lower NI after feeding balanced rations resulted in lower N excretion and, thus, reduction in N2O emissions from the manure. The reduction in manure N excretion was due to improved N-use efficiency for milk production in COWS and buffaloes. Similar to our findings, Kaushal et al. (2011) also reported lower N excretion due to better N-use efficiency in dairy animals in India.

Manure-management practices at farm also influence emissions of CH4 and N20. For instance, under solid-storage manure-management system, less CH4 is formed due to an aerobic decomposition. According to the IPCC, the conversion rate of VS to CH4 in manure, for the same annual average temperature (17°C), in solid storage was 4% compared with 76% of the lagoon manure-management system. Nevertheless, a higher proportion of N2O will be emitted in this aerobic condition than that in the more anaerobic condition of the lagoon system (IPCC 2006).

78: In the present study, emissions of CH4 from enteric fermentation, CH4 from manure management, N2O from manure management and GHG (CO2, CH4 and N20) from feed production (on the basis of economic allocation to feed)

contributed 69.9%, 6.3%, 9.6% and 14.2% in COWS and 71.6%, 7.4%, 12.6% and 8.4% in buffaloes respectively, to the baseline (before RB) lifetime total GHG emissions. The current LCA study indicated that the largest contributing GHG emissions were from enteric fermentation in smallholder dairy production systems of India. Various studies have also reported that CH4 is the largest contributor to the GHG intensity of milk (45-70%; Beukes et al. 2010; de Vries and de Boer 2010; Bell et al. 2011). Of this, >75% is derived directly from enteric fermentation (Rotz et al. 2010). Therefore, it is apparent that abatement of enteric CH4 would result in the most significant reduction in Ei of milk from smallholder dairy production system.

So far, very few studies have been conducted on Ei of smallholder dairy production systems in developing countries. In LCA study of smallholder dairying in Kenya, Weiler et al. (2014) reported that the average Ei of milk (on the basis of economic allocation to milk and meat) was 2.0 kg CO2-eq/kg milk, whereas the Ei of milk with economic allocation (economic allocation to milk, meat, manure as fertiliser, cattle as a means of financing and insurance) was 1.6 kg CO2-eq/kg milk. Weiler et al. (2014) also reported that the emissions from enteric fermentation, feed production and manure deposit on pasture contributed 68%, 16% and 15% respectively, of the total emissions. An LCA conducted in Iran reported an average Ei of 1.57 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM at farm gate, and 1.73 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM at the milk-processing gate, indicating that ~90% of the emissions were from the raw milk production (Daneshi et al. 2014).

Many LCA studies conducted on Ei of milk production, mostly in European countries, have reported similar Ei at the farm gate, ranging from ~0.9 to 1.5 kg CO2-eq/kg milk (Thoma et al. 2013). Opio et al. (2013) reported that Ei (kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM) of milk varies from 1.6 in the eastern and western Europe to 9.0 in sub-Saharan Africa. They also reported that, generally, industrialised regions of the world exhibit the lowest Ei (1.6-1.7 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM), while, in developing regions, the range of Ei for milk is wider (2.0-9.0 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM). Our results of Ei (kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM) during a lifetime milk production, on the basis of economic, mass and digestible allocations, were lower, being 1.6, 1.8 and 1.7 in a cow and 2.3, 2.5 and 2.4 in a buffalo respectively, before ration balancing, than the suggested Ei of 5.5 for cow milk and 3.2 for buffalo milk reported by the FAO for southern Asia (Gerber et al. 2013). After feeding balanced rations, average Ei (kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM) of milk production can be further reduced to 1.1, 1.2 and 1.2 in a cow, and to 1.5, 1.6 and 1.5 in a buffalo. Variation between the Ei of milk as reported in the present study and that reported by Opio et al. (2013) could be due to the difference in the sources of GHG emissions, manure-management systems, feed digestibility and milk-production data used by Opio et al. (2013). Global studies by Opio et al. (2013) considered N20 emissions from direct deposition of manure by grazing stock and scavenging animals, indirect CO2 emissions from energy used during the manufacture of materials for on-farm structures (building and equipment) and CO2 emissions due to direct on-farm energy used for livestock (e.g. cooling, ventilation and heating). Almost all the animals in our studied areas were stall fed and housed in low

input-based sheds built using mainly wooden planks and asbestos sheets. In addition, there was no on-farm cooling of milk; instead, the milk was immediately carried to the village- level dairy cooperative societies where bulk milk coolers were made available by the district milk union. Therefore, contribution of emissions of CO2 and N20 from these sources could be less for the smallholder system in the present study. In southern Asia, only 2.0% of the dairy cattle manure and 19.9% of the dairy buffalo manure was managed in solid form and the remainder was managed in various systems, such as burned for fuel, daily spread, deposited on pasture and liquid (Opio et al. (2013); in contrast, 100% of the manure from cattle and buffalo was managed as solid storage in our study. Therefore, CH4 and N20 emissions from manure management could vary between southern Asia and our study. An estimated average feed digestibility used by Opio et al. (2013) was 52.6% and 52.1% for dairy cow and buffalo respectively, whereas, in the present study, national average feed digestibility was estimated at 57.1% for cows (before and after RB), and 55.4% and 56.3% for buffaloes (before and after RB respectively). Lower digestibility of feed was reported to be associated with higher enteric CH4 emissions (Mangino et al. 2003), and, thereby, higher carbon footprint of milk. Another reason for the difference between our results and those of the global studies could be the higher annual average milk production of cows (2926 kg before RB and 3054 kg after RB) and buffalo (2342 kg before RB and 2424 kg after RB) in our studied areas, whereas Opio et al. (2013) reported an overall annual average of ~1000 kg/cow for southern Asia. Milk-production levels in global studies used statistical data that were based on all cattle and official market channels of milk in specific countries (Weiler et al. 2014) and, therefore, these data could vary from the actual milk-production data of smallholder farms.

It has been found that there is an inverse relationship between milk yield and Ei of milk (Gerber et al. 2011). In our study, feeding balanced rations improved milk yield of dairy cows and buffaloes, which then helped reduce the Ei of milk. The present study showed that increasing milk yield from 1300 to 4000 kg of FPCM/lactation of a cow and buffalo (pooled data) causes a significant (P < 0.01, R2 = 0.74) reduction in emission intensity (Fig. 2). Our results are in agreement with those reported by
Fig. 2. Correlation of fat and protein-corrected milk (FPCM) yield and greenhouse gas (GHG) emission intensity.

Zehetmeier et al. (2014). This decreased Ei with increased milk yield is achieved mainly through increased efficiency caused by a dilution of maintenance energy requirements per kilogram of milk (Place and Mitloehner 2010). Gerber et al. (2011) and Zehetmeier et al. (2014) reported that increasing milk yield from 2000 to 5000 kg of FPCM/cow.year causes a large reduction in Ei, whereas Ei moves towards a plateau from 6000 kg of FPCM/cow.year onward. Zehetmeier et al. (2014) also reported that there is a smaller reduction in Ei in high-yielding dairy cow production systems, where the additional benefit from increasing milk yield is limited.

Our results showed that smallholder dairy production systems of India, where the average productivity of dairy animals is low to medium, provide a good opportunity to reduce Ei with improving productivity, through feeding a nutritionally balanced ration to dairy animals. Kebreab et al. (2001) and Rotz et al. (2010) also stated that there is considerable potential for improvement in milk production and reduction of GHG emissions from dairy animals through dietary manipulation. Improved efficiency of nutrient utilisation would increase productivity and reduce GHG emissions from animals. In addition to increased productivity, improved feed conversion efficiency can be a highly effective way of reducing CO2-eq emissions per kilogram of milk (Vellinga et al. 2011). Diet manipulation is the most direct, and arguably the most effective, means of lowering the GHG intensity of milk for intensive dairy operations because the diet composition directly affects CH4 emissions. The improvement in feed conversion efficiency (from 0.6 to 0.8 in COWS and from 0.6 to 0.7 in buffaloes) through feeding balanced rations under field conditions was also reported in India (Garg et al. 2013). Thus, increased productivity of COWS and buffaloes offers considerable mitigation potential for dairy production systems characterised by low productivity in India and other developing countries.

Diet manipulation is the most direct, and arguably the most effective, means of lowering the GHG intensity of milk for intensive dairy operations because the diet composition directly affects CH4 emissions. The improvement in feed conversion efficiency (from 0.6 to 0.8 in COWS and from 0.6 to 0.7 in buffaloes) through feeding balanced rations under field conditions was also reported in India (Garg et al. 2013). Thus, increased productivity of COWS and buffaloes offers considerable mitigation potential for dairy production systems characterised by low productivity in India and other developing countries.
80: In the present study, emissions of CH4 from enteric fermentation, CH4 from manure management, N2O from manure management and GHG (CO2, CH4 and N20) from feed production (on the basis of economic allocation to feed)

contributed 69.9%, 6.3%, 9.6% and 14.2% in COWS and 71.6%, 7.4%, 12.6% and 8.4% in buffaloes respectively, to the baseline (before RB) lifetime total GHG emissions. The current LCA study indicated that the largest contributing GHG emissions were from enteric fermentation in smallholder dairy production systems of India. Various studies have also reported that CH4 is the largest contributor to the GHG intensity of milk (45-70%; Beukes et al. 2010; de Vries and de Boer 2010; Bell et al. 2011). Of this, >75% is derived directly from enteric fermentation (Rotz et al. 2010). Therefore, it is apparent that abatement of enteric CH4 would result in the most significant reduction in Ei of milk from smallholder dairy production system.

So far, very few studies have been conducted on Ei of smallholder dairy production systems in developing countries. In LCA study of smallholder dairying in Kenya, Weiler et al. (2014) reported that the average Ei of milk (on the basis of economic allocation to milk and meat) was 2.0 kg CO2-eq/kg milk, whereas the Ei of milk with economic allocation (economic allocation to milk, meat, manure as fertiliser, cattle as a means of financing and insurance) was 1.6 kg CO2-eq/kg milk. Weiler et al. (2014) also reported that the emissions from enteric fermentation, feed production and manure deposit on pasture contributed 68%, 16% and 15% respectively, of the total emissions. An LCA conducted in Iran reported an average Ei of 1.57 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM at farm gate, and 1.73 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM at the milk-processing gate, indicating that ~90% of the emissions were from the raw milk production (Daneshi et al. 2014).

Many LCA studies conducted on Ei of milk production, mostly in European countries, have reported similar Ei at the farm gate, ranging from ~0.9 to 1.5 kg CO2-eq/kg milk (Thoma et al. 2013). Opio et al. (2013) reported that Ei (kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM) of milk varies from 1.6 in the eastern and western Europe to 9.0 in sub-Saharan Africa. They also reported that, generally, industrialised regions of the world exhibit the lowest Ei (1.6-1.7 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM), while, in developing regions, the range of Ei for milk is wider (2.0-9.0 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM). Our results of Ei (kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM) during a lifetime milk production, on the basis of economic, mass and digestible allocations, were lower, being 1.6, 1.8 and 1.7 in a cow and 2.3, 2.5 and 2.4 in a buffalo respectively, before ration balancing, than the suggested Ei of 5.5 for cow milk and 3.2 for buffalo milk reported by the FAO for southern Asia (Gerber et al. 2013). After feeding balanced rations, average Ei (kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM) of milk production can be further reduced to 1.1, 1.2 and 1.2 in a cow, and to 1.5, 1.6 and 1.5 in a buffalo. Variation between the Ei of milk as reported in the present study and that reported by Opio et al. (2013) could be due to the difference in the sources of GHG emissions, manure-management systems, feed digestibility and milk-production data used by Opio et al. (2013). Global studies by Opio et al. (2013) considered N20 emissions from direct deposition of manure by grazing stock and scavenging animals, indirect CO2 emissions from energy used during the manufacture of materials for on-farm structures (building and equipment) and CO2 emissions due to direct on-farm energy used for livestock (e.g. cooling, ventilation and heating). Almost all the animals in our studied areas were stall fed and housed in low

input-based sheds built using mainly wooden planks and asbestos sheets. In addition, there was no on-farm cooling of milk; instead, the milk was immediately carried to the village- level dairy cooperative societies where bulk milk coolers were made available by the district milk union. Therefore, contribution of emissions of CO2 and N20 from these sources could be less for the smallholder system in the present study. In southern Asia, only 2.0% of the dairy cattle manure and 19.9% of the dairy buffalo manure was managed in solid form and the remainder was managed in various systems, such as burned for fuel, daily spread, deposited on pasture and liquid (Opio et al. (2013); in contrast, 100% of the manure from cattle and buffalo was managed as solid storage in our study. Therefore, CH4 and N20 emissions from manure management could vary between southern Asia and our study. An estimated average feed digestibility used by Opio et al. (2013) was 52.6% and 52.1% for dairy cow and buffalo respectively, whereas, in the present study, national average feed digestibility was estimated at 57.1% for cows (before and after RB), and 55.4% and 56.3% for buffaloes (before and after RB respectively). Lower digestibility of feed was reported to be associated with higher enteric CH4 emissions (Mangino et al. 2003), and, thereby, higher carbon footprint of milk. Another reason for the difference between our results and those of the global studies could be the higher annual average milk production of cows (2926 kg before RB and 3054 kg after RB) and buffalo (2342 kg before RB and 2424 kg after RB) in our studied areas, whereas Opio et al. (2013) reported an overall annual average of ~1000 kg/cow for southern Asia. Milk-production levels in global studies used statistical data that were based on all cattle and official market channels of milk in specific countries (Weiler et al. 2014) and, therefore, these data could vary from the actual milk-production data of smallholder farms.

It has been found that there is an inverse relationship between milk yield and Ei of milk (Gerber et al. 2011). In our study, feeding balanced rations improved milk yield of dairy cows and buffaloes, which then helped reduce the Ei of milk. The present study showed that increasing milk yield from 1300 to 4000 kg of FPCM/lactation of a cow and buffalo (pooled data) causes a significant (P < 0.01, R2 = 0.74) reduction in emission intensity (Fig. 2). Our results are in agreement with those reported by
Fig. 2. Correlation of fat and protein-corrected milk (FPCM) yield and greenhouse gas (GHG) emission intensity.

Zehetmeier et al. (2014). This decreased Ei with increased milk yield is achieved mainly through increased efficiency caused by a dilution of maintenance energy requirements per kilogram of milk (Place and Mitloehner 2010). Gerber et al. (2011) and Zehetmeier et al. (2014) reported that increasing milk yield from 2000 to 5000 kg of FPCM/cow.year causes a large reduction in Ei, whereas Ei moves towards a plateau from 6000 kg of FPCM/cow.year onward. Zehetmeier et al. (2014) also reported that there is a smaller reduction in Ei in high-yielding dairy cow production systems, where the additional benefit from increasing milk yield is limited.

Our results showed that smallholder dairy production systems of India, where the average productivity of dairy animals is low to medium, provide a good opportunity to reduce Ei with improving productivity, through feeding a nutritionally balanced ration to dairy animals. Kebreab et al. (2001) and Rotz et al. (2010) also stated that there is considerable potential for improvement in milk production and reduction of GHG emissions from dairy animals through dietary manipulation. Zehetmeier et al. (2014) also reported that there is a smaller reduction in Ei in high-yielding dairy cow production systems, where the additional benefit from increasing milk yield is limited.

Our results showed that smallholder dairy production systems of India, where the average productivity of dairy animals is low to medium, provide a good opportunity to reduce Ei with improving productivity, through feeding a nutritionally balanced ration to dairy animals. Kebreab et al. (2001) and Rotz et al. (2010) also stated that there is considerable potential for improvement in milk production and reduction of GHG emissions from dairy animals through dietary manipulation. Improved efficiency of nutrient utilisation would increase productivity and reduce GHG emissions from animals. In addition to increased productivity, improved feed conversion efficiency can be a highly effective way of reducing CO2-eq emissions per kilogram of milk (Vellinga et al. 2011). Diet manipulation is the most direct, and arguably the most effective, means of lowering the GHG intensity of milk for intensive dairy operations because the diet composition directly affects CH4 emissions. The improvement in feed conversion efficiency (from 0.6 to 0.8 in COWS and from 0.6 to 0.7 in buffaloes) through feeding balanced rations under field conditions was also reported in India (Garg et al. 2013). Thus, increased productivity of COWS and buffaloes offers considerable mitigation potential for dairy production systems characterised by low productivity in India and other developing countries.
84: In the present study, emissions of CH4 from enteric fermentation, CH4 from manure management, N2O from manure management and GHG (CO2, CH4 and N20) from feed production (on the basis of economic allocation to feed)

contributed 69.9%, 6.3%, 9.6% and 14.2% in COWS and 71.6%, 7.4%, 12.6% and 8.4% in buffaloes respectively, to the baseline (before RB) lifetime total GHG emissions. The current LCA study indicated that the largest contributing GHG emissions were from enteric fermentation in smallholder dairy production systems of India. Various studies have also reported that CH4 is the largest contributor to the GHG intensity of milk (45-70%; Beukes et al. 2010; de Vries and de Boer 2010; Bell et al. 2011). Of this, >75% is derived directly from enteric fermentation (Rotz et al. 2010). Therefore, it is apparent that abatement of enteric CH4 would result in the most significant reduction in Ei of milk from smallholder dairy production system.

So far, very few studies have been conducted on Ei of smallholder dairy production systems in developing countries. In LCA study of smallholder dairying in Kenya, Weiler et al. (2014) reported that the average Ei of milk (on the basis of economic allocation to milk and meat) was 2.0 kg CO2-eq/kg milk, whereas the Ei of milk with economic allocation (economic allocation to milk, meat, manure as fertiliser, cattle as a means of financing and insurance) was 1.6 kg CO2-eq/kg milk. Weiler et al. (2014) also reported that the emissions from enteric fermentation, feed production and manure deposit on pasture contributed 68%, 16% and 15% respectively, of the total emissions. An LCA conducted in Iran reported an average Ei of 1.57 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM at farm gate, and 1.73 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM at the milk-processing gate, indicating that ~90% of the emissions were from the raw milk production (Daneshi et al. 2014).

Many LCA studies conducted on Ei of milk production, mostly in European countries, have reported similar Ei at the farm gate, ranging from ~0.9 to 1.5 kg CO2-eq/kg milk (Thoma et al. 2013). Opio et al. (2013) reported that Ei (kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM) of milk varies from 1.6 in the eastern and western Europe to 9.0 in sub-Saharan Africa. They also reported that, generally, industrialised regions of the world exhibit the lowest Ei (1.6-1.7 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM), while, in developing regions, the range of Ei for milk is wider (2.0-9.0 kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM). Our results of Ei (kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM) during a lifetime milk production, on the basis of economic, mass and digestible allocations, were lower, being 1.6, 1.8 and 1.7 in a cow and 2.3, 2.5 and 2.4 in a buffalo respectively, before ration balancing, than the suggested Ei of 5.5 for cow milk and 3.2 for buffalo milk reported by the FAO for southern Asia (Gerber et al. 2013). After feeding balanced rations, average Ei (kg CO2-eq/kg FPCM) of milk production can be further reduced to 1.1, 1.2 and 1.2 in a cow, and to 1.5, 1.6 and 1.5 in a buffalo. Variation between the Ei of milk as reported in the present study and that reported by Opio et al. (2013) could be due to the difference in the sources of GHG emissions, manure-management systems, feed digestibility and milk-production data used by Opio et al. (2013). Global studies by Opio et al. (2013) considered N20 emissions from direct deposition of manure by grazing stock and scavenging animals, indirect CO2 emissions from energy used during the manufacture of materials for on-farm structures (building and equipment) and CO2 emissions due to direct on-farm energy used for livestock (e.g. cooling, ventilation and heating). Almost all the animals in our studied areas were stall fed and housed in low

input-based sheds built using mainly wooden planks and asbestos sheets. In addition, there was no on-farm cooling of milk; instead, the milk was immediately carried to the village- level dairy cooperative societies where bulk milk coolers were made available by the district milk union. Therefore, contribution of emissions of CO2 and N20 from these sources could be less for the smallholder system in the present study. In southern Asia, only 2.0% of the dairy cattle manure and 19.9% of the dairy buffalo manure was managed in solid form and the remainder was managed in various systems, such as burned for fuel, daily spread, deposited on pasture and liquid (Opio et al. (2013); in contrast, 100% of the manure from cattle and buffalo was managed as solid storage in our study. Therefore, CH4 and N20 emissions from manure management could vary between southern Asia and our study. An estimated average feed digestibility used by Opio et al. (2013) was 52.6% and 52.1% for dairy cow and buffalo respectively, whereas, in the present study, national average feed digestibility was estimated at 57.1% for cows (before and after RB), and 55.4% and 56.3% for buffaloes (before and after RB respectively). Lower digestibility of feed was reported to be associated with higher enteric CH4 emissions (Mangino et al. 2003), and, thereby, higher carbon footprint of milk. Another reason for the difference between our results and those of the global studies could be the higher annual average milk production of cows (2926 kg before RB and 3054 kg after RB) and buffalo (2342 kg before RB and 2424 kg after RB) in our studied areas, whereas Opio et al. (2013) reported an overall annual average of ~1000 kg/cow for southern Asia. Milk-production levels in global studies used statistical data that were based on all cattle and official market channels of milk in specific countries (Weiler et al. 2014) and, therefore, these data could vary from the actual milk-production data of smallholder farms.

It has been found that there is an inverse relationship between milk yield and Ei of milk (Gerber et al. 2011). In our study, feeding balanced rations improved milk yield of dairy cows and buffaloes, which then helped reduce the Ei of milk. The present study showed that increasing milk yield from 1300 to 4000 kg of FPCM/lactation of a cow and buffalo (pooled data) causes a significant (P < 0.01, R2 = 0.74) reduction in emission intensity (Fig. 2). Our results are in agreement with those reported by
Fig. 2. Correlation of fat and protein-corrected milk (FPCM) yield and greenhouse gas (GHG) emission intensity.

Zehetmeier et al. (2014). This decreased Ei with increased milk yield is achieved mainly through increased efficiency caused by a dilution of maintenance energy requirements per kilogram of milk (Place and Mitloehner 2010). Gerber et al. (2011) and Zehetmeier et al. (2014) reported that increasing milk yield from 2000 to 5000 kg of FPCM/cow.year causes a large reduction in Ei, whereas Ei moves towards a plateau from 6000 kg of FPCM/cow.year onward. Zehetmeier et al. (2014) also reported that there is a smaller reduction in Ei in high-yielding dairy cow production systems, where the additional benefit from increasing milk yield is limited.

Our results showed that smallholder dairy production systems of India, where the average productivity of dairy animals is low to medium, provide a good opportunity to reduce Ei with improving productivity, through feeding a nutritionally balanced ration to dairy animals. Kebreab et al. (2001) and Rotz et al. (2010) also stated that there is considerable potential for improvement in milk production and reduction of GHG emissions from dairy animals through dietary manipulation. Improved efficiency of nutrient utilisation would increase productivity and reduce GHG emissions from animals. In addition to increased productivity, improved feed conversion efficiency can be a highly effective way of reducing CO2-eq emissions per kilogram of milk (Vellinga et al. 2011). Diet manipulation is the most direct, and arguably the most effective, means of lowering the GHG intensity of milk for intensive dairy operations because the diet composition directly affects CH4 emissions. The improvement in feed conversion efficiency (from 0.6 to 0.8 in COWS and from 0.6 to 0.7 in buffaloes) through feeding balanced rations under field conditions was also reported in India (Garg et al. 2013). Thus, increased productivity of COWS and buffaloes offers considerable mitigation potential for dairy production systems characterised by low productivity in India and other developing countries.
85: The results of the present study showed that, on average, 1.6 (for cow milk) and 2.3 (for buffalo milk) kg CO2-eq GHG are emitted per kilogram of fat and protein corrected lifetime milk produced under traditional feeding practices in smallholder dairy production systems of India. After feeding nutritionally balanced rations, emission intensity of milk on lifetime basis reduces by 31.2% and 34.7% in COWS and buffaloes, respectively. An LCA study also showed that emissions of enteric fermentation contribute the highest to overall GHG emissions in milk chain at the farm-gate level. The implementation of a balanced-nutrition approach in smallholder mixed dairying systems of India, using locally available feed resources and mineral mixture, improves nutrient utilisation and milk production, and reduces emissions of enteric methane, manure methane and manure nitrous oxide. The results of our study demonstrated that improving productivity of dairy animals throughout their lifespan by using balanced-diet approach could be a one of the promising strategies for reducing emission intensity in smallholder systems. A major conclusion of the present study is that there is an opportunity to reduce emission intensity through feeding
balanced rations. This study may serve as a benchmark for future studies of the smallholder dairy systems in India as well as in other developing countries.

balanced rations. This study may serve as a benchmark for future studies of the smallholder dairy systems in India as well as in other developing countries.
